coredata.dc:title: Novel ternary catalyst Ag2O/NCDs@ porous tubular g-C3N4 as efficient visible-light-driven peroxymonosulfate activator
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Weifang
Huang, Yanchun
Yang, Lingxiao
Li, Jun
Liu, Chao
Li, Naiwen
Lai, Bo
coredata.dc:description: The photo-induced peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation method for the removal of emerging pollutants is regarded as a promising technique, and designing an effective and stable catalyst is the key. In the current work, a novel Ag2O nanoparticle and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified porous tubular carbon nitride (Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN) catalyst was synthesized. The morphology, composition, and optical activities of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN were analyzed by TEM, XRD, XPS, and UV–vis, respectively. Catalytic degradation test results showed that the degradation efficiency of carbamazepine by Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS (0.1452 min−1) was greatly enhanced compared with Vis-PMS (0.0014 min−1). A series of mechanistic analysis indicated that both non-radical and radical played an important role in (1O2, h+, and O2 •-) carbamazepine degradation. In addition, the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system showed low silver ion leaching, high adaptability to solution pH, and low toxicity of degradation products. This research may provide a green and hopeful practical application potential for degrading the persistent organic pollutants in the aquatic environment.

head.ce:title: Novel ternary catalyst Ag2O/NCDs@ porous tubular g-C3N4 as efficient visible-light-driven peroxymonosulfate activator

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photo-induced peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation method for the removal of emerging pollutants is regarded as a promising technique, and designing an effective and stable catalyst is the key. In the current work, a novel Ag2O nanoparticle and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified porous tubular carbon nitride (Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN) catalyst was synthesized. The morphology, composition, and optical activities of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN were analyzed by TEM, XRD, XPS, and UV–vis, respectively. Catalytic degradation test results showed that the degradation efficiency of carbamazepine by Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS (0.1452 min−1) was greatly enhanced compared with Vis-PMS (0.0014 min−1). A series of mechanistic analysis indicated that both non-radical and radical played an important role in (1O2, h+, and O2 •-) carbamazepine degradation. In addition, the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system showed low silver ion leaching, high adaptability to solution pH, and low toxicity of degradation products. This research may provide a green and hopeful practical application potential for degrading the persistent organic pollutants in the aquatic environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) as emerging pollutants in the water environment have gotten more and more attention due to the possible damage it poses to aquatic life and human health. [1,2]. Carbamazepine (CBZ) as a common psychiatric drug was a commonly detected pharmaceutical due to its low biodegradability, which has already been observed in surface water (3.09 μg/L), groundwater (610 ng/L), and drinking water (30 ng/L) [3,4]. CBZ has been found to be toxic to bacteria, algae, fish, and even the shape and reproductive capabilities of human embryonic cells in water [5]. It was difficult to completely remove CBZ by traditional treatment technologies, including ozone oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, and biodegradation; thus, it was crucial to develop an effective technology for the removal of CBZ from water bodies and wastewater [6].

Over the past decades, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), such as photocatalysis oxidation, Fenton oxidation, and Fenton-like oxidation, etc., have shown to be one of the most effective and promising methods, which generated highly oxidizing active including free radicals and non-radical species to regrade the refractory pollutants [7]. Peroxymonosulfate (PMS), as a favorable alternative to peroxy-disulfate (PDS) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), has received increasing interest because of its ability to generate both HO• and SO4 •- radicals [8], as well as its improved adaptability across a wide pH range. Numerous methods for PMS activation have been proposed, such as transition metals (e.g., Cu, Ag, Co, Mo) [9–11], ultraviolet (UV) [12], ultrasound [13], carbon-based catalysts [14–16], and so on. Meanwhile, as one of the mainstream AOPs, photocatalytic oxidation using sun energy directly has been extensively researched to degrade refractory organic compounds. However, photocatalytic oxidation had the disadvantage of the catalyst’s high electron-hole recombination rate induced by light, resulting in a low reaction process. According to many studies, SO4 •- could be easily produced by the synergistic activation under the light irradiation and PMS presence as the Vis-PMS system, which was regarded as an efficient approach to removing many refractory organic pollutants [17].

The photocatalysis community has paid close attention to the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) semiconductor owing to its acceptable bandgap (2.7 eV), inexpensive price, and good thermal and chemical stability [18]. But even if, the practical photocatalytic application of g-C3N4 remains constrained by its fast charge recombination, low electrical conductivity, and weak visible light absorption (<460 nm). To address the above tasks, different strategies such as element doping, combination with conductive materials, constructing vacancy, and manipulation of morphology and structure have been proposed to get over the limitations of pure g-C3N4 [19]. According to recent experimental and theoretical studies, the one-dimensional (1D) tubular g-C3N4 materials, which have higher photocatalytic activity than pure g-C3N4 [20], can be synthesized by a facile pyrolysis treatment in an N2 atmosphere [21]. For illustration, Wang et al. evaluated the elimination of the antibiotic tetracycline hydrochloride using tubular and sheet g-C3N4 and discovered that the degradation efficiency of tubular g-C3N4 photocatalyst was twice as high as that of sheet g-C3N4 [22].

The construction of heterojunctions consisting of g-C3N4 and semiconductors, such as Zn-compounds [23], Ag-compounds [24], and Mo-compounds [25], has been demonstrated to be a potent and successful method for increasing the activity of g-C3N4. Among them, it was noted that the photocatalytic activity of these catalysts greatly increased when g-C3N4 was coupled with Ag-based catalysts, such as Ag2O [26–28] and Ag3PO4 [29]. Li et al. [29] prepared a novel Ag3PO4-modified tubular carbon nitride that could degrade 8.5 µM carbamazepine in 30 min (0.0927 min−1). In the experiment, the concentration of silver ions in the system was 7.06 mg/L, which was 70 times the maximum allowable concentration in drinking water recommended by the World Health Organization. It would cause harm to human health. Therefore, how to prepare a stable low dissolution silver carbon nitride catalyst is the focus of research. Ag2O with a narrower energy band gap (1.2 eV) possessed stronger photo-oxidative ability [30], which modified g-C3N4 and exhibited an extremely effective photocatalyst [26]. Nevertheless, the problems of insufficient visible light utilization, low stability, and the high silver dissolution rate have not been resolved yet.

N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) doped have been frequently used to modify semiconductors to enhance photocatalytic performance [31]. It is worth noting that NCDs can not only utilize their excellent conversion photoluminescence (PL) performance to improve the utilization of visible light but also can suppress the recombination of photo-separated charges. Therefore, we deduced that the loading of NCDs on Ag2O@PTCN could result in a stable, efficient, and low silver ion solubilized catalyst with good degradation performance by activating PMS under visible light.

In this study, the ternary catalyst prepared by NCDs, Ag2O nanoparticles, and porous tubular g-C3N4 (PTCN) as the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite was conducted to effectively degrade the carbamazepine (CBZ) in the Vis-PMS system. The main objectives of this work were to (1) synthesize ternary catalyst and analyze the optical properties by characterization tests; (2) study Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN’s catalytic activity and Ag+ leaching in the Vis-PMS system; (3) the effect of pivotal operating parameters (Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN dosage, PMS concentration, and pH) on degradation; (3) identify the dominant ROS in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system and propose the mechanism and path of CBZ degradation; (4) evaluate the practical application potential and the stability of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN for refractory organic compounds degradation in water.

In Supplementary Material Table S1, a list of all chemicals utilized was provided. All chemicals were directly used as receiving solutions without any further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of tubular porous carbon nitride (PTCN)
ce:section.ce:para: The porous tubular g-C3N4 (PTCN) was prepared by a simple calcination approach [29]. Typically, 14.4 g of urea and 1.2 g of melamine powder were poured into a crucible (50 mL) with a cover in a muffle furnace in the air under the condition of temperature increasing to 180 ℃ at a heating rate of 2.5 ℃/min and hold for 30 min. Closely followed by a further improving temperature to 500 ℃ at a heating rate of 2.5 ℃/min and hold for 2 h. Finally, the product was taken out and grounded in an agate mortar to obtain a faint yellow powder for further use, which was refined as PTCN.

Ag2O@PTCN was fabricated through the deposition method [28] and a detailed procedure was shown in Text S1. According to the different proportions of Ag2O in the mass of PTCN (5 %, 10 %, 20 %, 30 %) in the preparation process, the prepared samples were recorded as 5 %Ag2O@PTCN, 10 %Ag2O@PTCN, 20 %Ag2O@PTCN, and 30 %Ag2O@PTCN. According to CBZ degradation experiment findings in the Vis-PMS system (Fig. S1), 5 %Ag2O@TPCN composite material had the strongest degradation of CBZ, which could be completely degraded in 30 min, and the other 10 %, 20 %, and 30 % degradation rates reach 92.4 %, 89.3 %, 84.7 %, respectively. Therefore, 5 %Ag2O@TPCN composite material was selected when preparing ternary catalytic materials with NCDs.

The NCDs aqueous dispersions were obtained by hydrothermal method [32]. First, 3.0 g citric acid and 1.0 g glycine were dispersed in 30 mL deionized water and mixed evenly. Then, the mixed solution was put in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel container and maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. The obtained solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to separate any large particles. After collection, the obtained supernatant was freeze-dried to obtain NCDs powder.

Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN was prepared by a typical synthesis process based on previous studies [32]. The preparation process was described in Supplementary Materials Text S2. The obtained composites were labeled as Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-2, Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-4, Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-8, Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-16 (Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN), and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-24, which loads of NCDs were 2, 4, 8, 16, and 24 mg, respectively. According to the results of CBZ degradation experiments in the Vis-PMS system, the ternary composites of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN-16 showed the highest degradation efficiency, and the degradation rate of CBZ can reach 99.9 % within 20 min. (Fig. S2). For comparison, Ag2O/NCDs and NCDs@PTCN were synthesized by the same route. The detailed procedures of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN are proposed in Scheme 1 .

The catalytic capacity of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN in the Vis-PMS system was assessed by degrading the target contaminant CBZ. A certain mass of catalyst material was added to 150 mL of CBZ solution at a concentration of 8.5 μM, sonicated and dispersed for 1 min, poured into a 300 mL jacketed beaker, and then stirred for 10 min in the dark. Following the addition of 0.45 mM PMS, the system was exposed to visible light to start the reaction. A Xenon lamp light source device (PLS-SXE300+, Beijing Perfect light) with wavelength cut-off filters (λ ≥ 420 nm) generated visible light. The reaction devices of the degradation experiment were showed in Fig. S3. During degradation experiments, 1 mL of sample solution was pumped into a 2 mL syringe with a 0.22 μm membrane filter at a given time interval, and quenched the degradation process by 50 μL Na2S2O3 solution (25 mM). Detailed experimental procedures for the initial pH of a solution, anion effects, quenching experiments, lifetime experiments, and practical application experiments were described in Text S3.

The concentration of targeted organic compounds was immediately measured by High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC). The detailed measurement methods for different targeted organic compounds by HPLC were listed in Table S2. The degradation intermediates of CBZ in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system were monitored by Ultraperformance liquid chromatography-quadrupole time of flight premier mass spectrometer (UPLC-QTOF-MS/MS, Agilent 6500, USA). The detailed analytical methods were shown in Supporting Information Text S4.

The microstructure of Ag2O, PTCN, 5 %Ag2O@PTCN, and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite materials was characterized by SEM and TEM. Fig. 1 (a) illustrated that PTCN was in the shape of porous hollow nanotubes, which indicated that porous carbon nitride nanotubes have been successfully prepared. According to Fig. 1(b), Ag2O was a regular spherical or ellipsoidal particle with a diameter of about 200 nm. Fig. 1(d) was the TEM image of the as-prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), which showed that it was monodispersed and had a narrow size distribution of 2.5–4.5 nm, which was in line with the literature findings [33]. As shown in Fig. 1(c), Ag2O particles loaded on the PTCN in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composites were not seen, and this was because at a lower addition ratio, Ag2O particles dispersed on the surface of PTCN without aggregating into large particles [29]. In addition, as shown in Fig. S4, Ag2O particles loaded on the PTCN in the 5 %Ag2O@PTCN and 10 %Ag2O@PTCN composites were not seen, however, when the Ag2O loading concentration was increased from 5 % to 30 %, some agglomerated spheres could be observed to adhere well to the PTCN. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscope (HAADF-TEM) (Fig. 1(e, f)) and element mapping of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN (Fig. 1(g)) showed the existence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and silver elements. In particular, it showed how oxygen and silver elements were uniformly dispersed in Ag2O that was adhered to the surface of PTCN. Additionally, in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite (Fig. 1(e, f)), certain small-sized NCDs were anchored to the surfaces of both PTCN and Ag2O, which might act as an electron mediator between Ag2O and PTCN and increase the rate of electron-hole pair separation during the photocatalytic process.

The phase, crystal structure, and crystal integrity of PTCN, Ag2O, 5 %Ag2O@PTCN, and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN were studied via XRD analysis. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the 27.5° peak of (002) [29] facing the graphite material could be observed in PTCN, which was the in-plane structural accumulation and in-plane accumulation signal of the aromatic system. The diffraction peaks of pure Ag2O at 32.95°, 38.26°, 55.23°, and 65.80° could be classified into (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of cubic structure (JCPDF # 75 – 1532) [34]. There was no obvious characteristic peak of Ag2O in the Ag2O@PTCN composite, which might be due to the low amount of Ag2O. In Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN, the content of NCDs was much lower than that of PTCN, which was related to the fact that no typical NCDs peak was seen in Fig. 2(a).

The FTIR spectra of the catalysts were shown in Fig. 2(b). For PTCN, the main peaks at 1200–1750 cm−1 and 3000–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the characteristic stretching mode of the CN heterocycles, the NH bond and OH bond physically absorbed water [22]. Several peaks at 1647 cm−1, 1425 cm−1, 1380 cm−1, and 3430 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of NCDs were attributed to CN, CO, NH, and C-OH and NH [35]. The FTIR spectra of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composites reflect the overlap of the spectra of the PTCN and Ag2O spectra, and a change in the chemical character of the surface of photocatalysts was observed after the deposition of NCDs. In Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composites, the wider band at ∼ 3430 cm−1 corresponded to C-OH and NH and the 1434 cm−1 peak became stronger. The NCDs were effectively coated onto the Ag2O@PTCN surface, according to these FT-IR results [35].

The surface chemical composition and chemical state of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite were determined by the XPS method presented in Fig. 2(c-f). From the full spectrum of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN in Fig. S5, its constituent elements and C, N, O, and Ag elements can be observed, which account for 48.13 %, 47.03 %, 3.17 %, and 1.68 % of the catalyst mass, respectively. The multi-peak fitting results of C1s in Fig. 2(c) showed that the characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.2 eV and 287.9 eV, which were assigned to CC bonds, hydroxylated surface carbon atoms (C-OH), and the sp 2 hybridized carbons bonded to N in the form of C-(N)3 in the heterocycle ring of NCDs and PTCN, respectively [36]. The multi-peak fitting spectra of N 1 s were shown in Fig. 2(d). In addition to the peaks at 398.1 eV and 399.1 eV, the NHx group in PTCN produced a peak at 400.1 eV [29], indicating the existence of PTCN in Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composites. Two distinct peaks were discovered in the high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2(e)) at 530.9 eV and 531.6 eV, which was due to the lattice of oxygen atoms in the Ag2O [37] and the other originated from the chemisorbed oxygen of surface hydroxyl (–OH)[38]. Fig. 2(f) showed that the peak of Ag3d is located at 366.1 eV and 371.2 eV, which is equivalent to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, corresponding to Ag+ in Ag2O. This result shows that there is no metallic Ag existed in Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN, and silver was loaded on PTCN in the form of Ag2O nanoparticles and formed heterojunctions. Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composites were successfully fabricated, according to the XPS results of the samples, which concurred with the XRD and FT-IR results.

The optical and photoelectrochemical activity of the as-prepared materials were monitored in order to investigate the underlying photocatalytic reaction mechanism in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system. The resistance in the separation and transmission of photoinduced electron and hole pairs was examined using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) method [39]. It was understood that the charge-transfer resistance on a material's surface would decrease with decreasing radius in an EIS Nyquist curve [40]. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the Nyquist semicircle of photocatalysts follows the order of Ag2O < Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN < Ag2O@PTCN < PTCN, indicating that Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite had a lower interface charge transfer resistance than pure PTCN and Ag2O@PTCN, which sped up the separation of photogenerated charge carriers in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system. The separation rate of photo-induced electron-hole pairs was further confirmed by steady-state PL spectroscopy in addition to EIS measurement [41]. The highest PL reactive intensity was observed in PTCN, suggesting the electron-hole pair recombination rate was the fastest (Fig. 3(b)). Ag2O and NCD decorating improved charge separation, as seen by the decreased PL intensities of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN and Ag2O@PTCN samples compared to PTCN [42]. Particularly, compared to Ag2O@PTCN, the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN demonstrated a greater efficiency for separating photo-excited electrons and holes.

The optical properties of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN, Ag2O@PTCN, Ag2O, and PTCN were characterized by the ultraviolet–visible solid diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) shown in Fig. 3(c). It was found that compared to pure PTCN and Ag2O@PTCN, the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite showed higher light adsorption capability and a longer absorption edge, which illustrated that adding NCDs to the system improves its ability to capture visible light. The strong visible-light absorption of NCDs (Fig. S6) stemmed from a variety of π-π* (CC) and n-π* (CO or CN) transitions [43,44].

The n value of g-C3N4 is related to the direct bandgap nature of sheet g-C3N4. In this study, due to the direct bandgap of PTCN, n is 2. The E g values of PTCN, Ag2O@PTCN, and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN could be estimated by Formula (1), which were 2.81 eV, 2.72 eV, and 2.64 eV respectively, as displayed in Fig. 3(d). As a result, the bandgap energy (Eg) of the composite Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN was lower than that of the pure PTCN and Ag2O@PTCN, demonstrating better visible light utilization and strengthened photocatalytic activity. The band structures of the PTCN, Ag2O@PTCN, and Ag2O@NCDs@PTCN materials studied by VB XPS were also shown in Fig. 3(e). As can be shown, the VB maxima of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite, Ag2O@PTCN, and PTCN were found to be approximately 1.71 eV, 1.36 eV, and 2.15 eV, respectively. This suggested that the addition of Ag2O and NCDs to the surface of PTCN might significantly affect the valence band structure of PTCN. The conduction band (CB) values could be calculated by the following formula [45]:

According to the above formula, the conduction band edge potential (ECB ) of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN, Ag2O@PTCN, and PTCN was determined to be −0.95 V, −1.36 V, and −0.66 V, respectively. In Fig. 3(f), the distinct band structure of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN, Ag2O@PTCN, and PTCN was supported.

According to Fig. 4 (a), the degradation rate of CBZ by Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN in the Vis-PMS system reached 99.9 % within 20 min, while the degradation rates of CBZ by NCDs@PTCN, Ag2O/NCDs, and Ag2O@PTCN were 49.6 %, 39.8 %, and 84.2 %, respectively. This indicated that the degradation effect of Ag2O@PTCN composite loaded with NCDs was significantly improved, which increased about 16.2 % higher than that of Ag2O@PTCN, 50.4 % higher than that of NCDs@PTCN, and 60.2 % higher than that of Ag2O/NCDs. From Fig. 4(b), the degradation rate was estimated by the Langmuir Hinshelwood model, and the reaction rate constants k obs of NCDs@PTCN, Ag2O/NCDs, and Ag2O@PTCN, for the degradation of CBZ in Vis-PMS system, were 0.0320 min−1, 0.0181 min−1, and 0.1020 min−1. The reaction rate constant kobs of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN for the degradation of CBZ in the Vis-PMS system were 0.1452 min−1, which were about 4.5, 8, and 1.4 times higher than those of binary composites, respectively. It could be found that Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN presented a higher reaction rate than Ag2O@PTCN in the degradation of CBZ in the Vis-PMS system. This was because the loading of NCDs as an electron transport medium hastened photogenerated electron and hole separation, therefore improving the catalytic performance. The results presented above illustrated that the photocatalytic oxidation capacity could be enhanced after loading Ag2O and NCDs on the surface of PTCN, thus effectively promoting the degradation of CBZ. As depicted in Table S3, both pollutant degradation efficiency and degradation rate in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system have great advantages for the contaminant elimination from water.

For comparison, the activities of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite for CBZ degradation under visible irradiation alone, PMS alone, and visible irradiation in the presence of PMS were evaluated with the same conditions, respectively. The results were presented in Fig. 4(c). It showed that in the Vis-PMS, Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis, and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/PMS systems, the removal rates of CBZ were very low, only 9.7 %, 38.4 %, and 7.2 %, respectively, indicating that the single visible light and single Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN catalyst could hardly effectively degrade the CBZ at the sole presence of PMS. However, the ternary Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS exhibited the excellent degradation efficiency of CBZ with 99.9 % within 20 min in the Vis-PMS system, which increased by 45.0 % over that of PTCN under the visible irradiation alone and 88 % of that PTCN at the PMS presence alone. According to Fig. 4(d), the apparent rate constant (kobs ) of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system was 0.1452 min−1, which was approximately 120 times greater than the Vis-PMS system (0.0014 min−1), 94 times greater than the apparent rate constant of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/PMS system (0.0018 min−1). According to the above results, it was confirmed that CBZ could be effectively degraded only when the visible light irradiation, Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN catalyst, and PMS existed simultaneously, indicating that there was an obvious synergistic enhancement effect for CBZ degradation in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration of Ag+ in solution after reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(e) demonstrated that Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS was more effective than Ag3PO4@TPCN12/Vis-PMS [29] in the removal of CBZ under the same conditions. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4(f), since the Ag2O loaded in Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN had the characteristics of less loading, more stable and smaller particles (Fig. S7), the leached Ag+ concentrations in solution following the reaction of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS were all lower than those of Ag2O@PTCN/Vis-PMS and Vis/10%Ag3PO4@TPCN12/Vis-PMS. It was worth noting, the Ag+ dissolution of Ag3PO4@TPCN12/Vis-PMS was 8.3 times that of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system, indicating that the prepared Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite is an environmentally friendly material.
Herein, some significant influencing elements including the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, and initial pH were examined in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system.

The effect of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN dosage on the CBZ removal rate with 0.45 mM PMS was investigated (Fig. 5 (a)). The degradation efficiency was greatly increased and almost all of the CBZ was degraded within 20 min with the dosage of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN rising from 0.1 g/L to 0.7 g/L. However, the k obs value of CBZ degradation had not greatly improved when further increasing the dosage to 0.3 g/L. Considering the reaction cost, when CBZ could be completely removed within 20 min when the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN dosage was 0.3 g/L. As a result, a catalyst dose of 0.3 g/L was chosen for further studies since it was shown to be the most effective.

To explore the influence of PMS concentration in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system on the degradation effect of CBZ, the optimal catalyst dosage (0.3 g/L) were adopted, and different concentrations of PMS (0.15 mM, 0.30 mM, 0.45 mM, 0.60 mM, 0.75 mM) were added to the reaction system, respectively. The degradation results of CBZ were shown in Fig. 5(b). The removal rate of CBZ increased from 94.9 % to 99.9 % in 20 min when the PMS concentration was increased from 0.30 mM to 0.45 mM, and the kobs improved from 0.1048 min−1 to 0.1452 min−1. As a consequence, the corresponding rate constant of CBZ degradation rose by 1.4 times, suggesting that the higher PMS concentration sped up the reactive sites on the surface of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN activation. The removal impact and removal rate of CBZ did not substantially change when PMS concentration was increased to 0.75 mM, which may be related to the restricted capacity of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN composite to activate PMS when exposed to visible light. As a result, 0.45 mM was determined to be the ideal PMS concentration for this investigation.

The pH of the reaction solution typically played an important role in AOPs, and it should be regarded as a crucial parameter for practical applications. When the pH value in the reaction system changes, it affects the morphology of organic compounds and the formation of active species thus affecting the degradation effect [46]. Fig. 5(c) described the use of H2SO4 and NaOH solutions to change the initial pH of the CBZ solution to typical pH values (3.0 – 9.0) on behalf of the acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions studied. Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN had different charge properties at different pH, in Fig S8 (a), the Zeta potential of pH < 6.4 was positive, thus affecting the adsorption of CBZ on Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN. It was found that the initial pH of 6.6 produced the highest level of catalytic activity, and the degradation rate of CBZ could reach 99.9 % within 20 min. However, the reaction was not favored by the acidic or basic conditions, particularly when the pH was high. When the initial pH value was 3.0, the removal rate of CBZ could reach 98.2 % within 30 min. When the initial pH value was 9 with the k obs value is 0.0551 min−1 (Fig. S8(b)) which was 2.6 times lower than the pH of 6.6 (0.1452 min−1), the removal rate of CBZ decreased to 81.4 % within 30 min. This indicated that when pH = 6.6, the electrostatic adsorption between CBZ and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN was enhanced, and the adsorption was the basis of the CBZ degradation process. According to a literature review, the pKa of the CBZ molecule was 2.3 and 13.9 respectively [47]. Due to the protonation of the NH2 group, CBZ tends to be positively charged at pH = 3–6.6, and negatively charged at pH = 9 due to the deprotonation of the amino group. Furthermore, the solution pH was monitored during the whole reaction process (Fig. 5(d)). The solution pH remained steady throughout the process when the initial pH was 3.0. Additionally, when the initial pH of the solution was 5.0, 6.6, and 9.0, the solution pH suddenly dropped to 3.7, 3.7, and 4.0 as a result of the quick dissolution of PMS, with no further significant changes occurring during the reaction phase. This resulted the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system adapting to a wider pH range.

To explore the mainly active radicals that led to CBZ degradation with the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN catalyst in the Vis-PMS system tertiary-butyl alcohol (TBA), TEMP, disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (Na2-EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ), and silver nitrate were employed to act as scavengers of HO•, SO4 •-, HO•, 1O2, O2 •-, hole (h+), and electron (e-). Ethanol is a powerful quencher for both HO• and SO4 •- at the same time. As present in Fig. 6 (a) and Table S4, the presence of the aforementioned inhibitors significantly inhibits the breakdown of CBZ, resulting in the removal of 1O2, O2 •-, (h+), and e- . The inhibitory rates of the agents were ≈94.0 %,≈99.2 %, ≈98.9 %, and 50.4 %. Nevertheless, as shown in Fig. S9, EPR signals of DMPO-SO4 •- and DMPO-HO• adducts were not found for the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN in the Vis-PMS system, which was the same as the results of the quenching experiment, indicating that the reactive species SO4 •-, and HO• had almost no effect on photocatalytic degradation. These results indicated that 1O2, O2 •-, and h+ played a key role in CBZ degradation.

Additionally, PMS alone and Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN systems did not exhibit a similar TEMP-1O2 adduct signal with a 1:1:1 characteristic peak, as seen in Fig. 6(c). Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system showed an obvious EPR signal of TEMP-1O2 adduct, which was explained by the possibility that photogenerated electrons from visible light irradiation could speed up the activation of PMS and conversion of SO4 •- into 1O2[48]. In the presence of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN with PMS alone, catalyst alone, or catalyst under visible light irradiation (Fig. 6(d)), no signal of DMPO-O2 •- was seen.

Moreover, the TEMP-1O2 peaks (Fig. 6(c)) and DMPO-O2 •- (Fig. 6(d)) over Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS were stronger than those over Ag2O/Vis-PMS, PTCN/Vis-PMS, Ag2O@PTCN/Vis-PMS. The increased 1O2 and O2 •- the intensity of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN suggested that NCDs could be added to increase molecular oxygen activation capacity. The EPR measurements of the four photocatalysts were completed to better understand the mechanism of degradation. No obvious signals were found in the EPR spectra of the catalyst, PMS, or catalyst when exposed to visible light, as shown in Fig. 6(e) and Fig. 6(f). Moreover, the TEMP-1O2 peaks (and DMPO-O2 •- over Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS were stronger than those over Ag2O/Vis-PMS, PTCN/Vis-PMS, Ag2O@PTCN/Vis-PMS. The higher 1O2 and O2 •-intensity of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN showed that the addition of NCDs could result in the increased molecular oxygen activation ability.

Based on the analyses above, a possible degradation process for the CBZ degradation was depicted in Fig. 7
. After irradiating, the PTCN, Ag2O, and NCDs could produce e- and h+ (Eq. (1)). The electrons in the CB of Ag2O transferred to PTCN and then recombined with O2 to generate O2 •– (Eq. (2)). Furthermore, O2 •– and SO4 •– could be converted into 1O2 (Eq. (3), (4), and (5)). On the one hand, h+ in the VB of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN was responsible for the direct oxidation of the CBZ. On the other hand, the interaction between h+ and the PMS may yield SO5 •–, which had a poor oxidation capability (Eq. (6)). A further transformation of SO5 •– to 1O2 (Eq. (7)) [49], was caused by the exciting holes, in addition to a transformation of SO5 •– to 1O2 that occurred when SO5 •– came into contact with (Eq. (8)). In conclusion, the active species h+, 1O2, and O2 •– work together to degrade CBZ into intermediates (Eq. (9)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Path of CBZ degradation and toxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: The possible CBZ degradation pathway in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system was proposed in Fig. 8(a) based on HPLC-MS results (Fig. S10). Following the formation of a carbon-centered radical cation on CBZ via a one-electron transfer [39], the hydration reaction was then carried out, resulting in the formation of two intermediates, P1 and P2. In order to complete the hydroxylation process and the synthesis of P1 (m/z 253), the reactive species that were produced in the system Vis-PMS specifically attacked the CH = CH on the core heterocyclic ring of CBZ.

Additionally, the hydroxylation and dihydroxylation processes might be used to oxidize the discovered hydroxyl-CBZ derivative (P1, m/z 253) into P4 (m/z 271), P3 (m/z 267), P5 (m/z 287), and P10 (m/z 255). In the meantime, reactive species' cleavage of the CH-CH covalent link on the identified hydroxyl-CBZ derivative P4 (m/z 271) produced P8 (m/z 269), which was followed by intermediates P10 (m/z 255) from hydroxylation. Along with the creation of P9 (m/z 180), which was then further oxidized to P11 (m/z 196), the product P6 (m/z 208) was also created by photochemically cleaving the acetyl group off P2 (m/z 253) by a ring contraction process. Through the ring-breaking reaction, P9, P11, and P10 could all be further degraded to create simpler compounds, which were then further oxidized and mineralized to produce H2O and CO2.

Based on the Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) study performed with the help of the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.), the ecotoxicity of CBZ and its degradation intermediates was evaluated. Fig. 8(b-d) showed Daphnia Magna's acute toxicity LC50, the bioaccumulation factor, the developmental toxicity, and the mutagenicity of CBZ and its breakdown products. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the LC50 of Daphnia Magna for CBZ was 3.56 mg/L, which was categorized as a “Toxic” level (the concentration of a chemical that causes 50 % of Daphnia Magna to die after 48 h). Products P4, P5, P6, and P7′s LC50 values, which were classified as “Harmful” levels (10–100 mg/L), range from 10.27 to 41.12 mg/L. Although seven additional equivalent levels were still deemed “toxic”, most LD50 values had dramatically increased. Fig. 8(c, d) showed that compared to the original CBZ, the majority of CBZ derivatives had lower bioaccumulation factors and mutagenicity. With the exception of P9 and P11, the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system could greatly lower the bioaccumulation factor of oxidation products (P1, P4, P5, P6, P7, and P10). Additionally, Fig. 8(d) showed that CBZ was a molecule with “Mutagenicity Positive”, but the four degradation products (P4, P7, P8, and P10) had “Mutagenicity Negative” status. The finding demonstrated that the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system exhibited high CBZ degradation capability as well as significantly reduced CBZ ecotoxicity.

In a real water environment, anions such as Cl-, NO3 –, HCO3 –, and H2PO4 - coexist in the water, which will affect the activity of catalysts and reduce the effect of pollutant removal. Therefore, sodium chloride, sodium bicarbonate, sodium nitrate, or monosodium phosphate were added to the reaction solution at different concentrations (0 mM, 1 mM, 5 mM, or 10 mM) to study their effects on the elimination of CBZ in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system. In Fig. 9 (a), It was discovered that the degradation efficiency noticeably decreased from 99.9 % to 73.3 % as the concentration of Cl increased from 0 to 10 mM. This was because the combination of Cl and the produced SO4 •-/HO• led to the formation of reactive species with a lower oxidation capacity, such as Cl•, HOCl•-, and Cl2 •-[50]. The addition of NO3 – (Fig. 9(b)) and HCO3 – (Fig. 9(c)) at different concentrations (1, 5, and 10 mM) into the degradation system resulted in a slight decrease in degradation efficiencies, which could be attributed to NO3 – (HCO3 –) and CBZ molecules competing for active sites on the surface of catalyst [51]. As seen in Fig. S9(a), the degradation of CBZ efficiency decreased from 99.9 % to 88.4 % when the concentration range of H2PO4 increased from 1 mM to 10 mM. This degradation may have been caused by the generation of low oxidizing HPO4 • after H2PO4 - reacted with SO4 •- and HO•, or it may have been caused by the deactivation of catalyst surface caused by H2PO4 - [54]. The above results showed that the degradation of CBZ in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system was slightly inhibited by Cl- and H2PO4 - anions, but CBZ was still completely degraded after 30 min. The system was almost unaffected by NO3 –, HCO3 –.

Ultrapure water, tap water, Jinjiang river water, and municipal sewage were employed as background water samples to further examine their performance in an actual water matrix. Table S5 provided the primary water quality metrics of these various matrices following filtration. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the degradation of CBZ in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system in various water matrices conformed to pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics, and the degradation rate decreased as water quality decreased from ultrapure water to municipal sewage, with values of 99.9 %, 81.0 %, 79.1 %, and 45.0 %. According to the above results, it was feasible to degrade CBZ in natural water bodies by the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system. The decomposition efficiency of three more organic compounds was employed as a target pollutant in order to examine the degradation effect of the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system on other developing contaminants and assess its potential for practical application. As shown in Fig. 9(e), SMX completely degraded in about 20 min, but ATZ and THIA degraded only considerably, with degradation efficiencies of 54.7 % and 53.3 % after 30 min, respectively. The results showed that the system degraded different pollutants selectively.

For the catalyst to be used in real applications, stability, and reusability was crucial factors. Therefore, the produced Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN visible light-activated PMS degradation of CBZ lifetime experiment was conducted to assess its reusability. The result that about 97.3 % of CBZ degradation efficiency was fulfilled in five continuous tests within 30 min (Fig. 9(f)) suggested that the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN catalyst had significant reaction activity in this heterogeneous photocatalytic oxidation process. The FT-IR, XRD, and XPS spectra were exhibited in Fig. S9(b-d), respectively, before and after the reaction was carried out. The phase structure of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN hardly changed in contrast to before and after the reaction.

In this study, a ternary composite catalyst (Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN) was constructed with silver oxide, nitrogen-modified carbon quantum dots, and porous tubular carbon nitride, and various characterizations confirmed its successful preparation. The Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN with PMS showed the excellent photocatalytic performance to degrade CBZ at a degradation rate of 99.9 % in 20 min with k obs of 0.1452 min−1, which was 1.5 times compared to Ag3PO4@TPCN12/Vis-PMS and Ag2O@PTCN/Vis-PMS. Moreover, the leached Ag+ concentrations in solution after the reaction of Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS were much low, which was only 0.12 times of Ag3PO4@TPCN12/Vis-PMS. The ROS scavenger experiments and EPR spectra demonstrated that 1O2, O2 ·-, and h+ were proven to be responsible for CBZ degradation. At the same time, the degradation path and toxicity assay of CBZ in the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system were explored. Following treatment with the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system, the acute toxicity LC50 of Daphnia Magna, the bioaccumulation factor, and the mutagenicity of breakdown products of CBZ were all reduced. And the Ag2O/NCDs@PTCN/Vis-PMS system had a wide range of pH adaptation, little influence of other coexisting anions on its degradation performance, high recyclability and stability, adaptable to CBZ removal in natural water, and had certain removal ability for SMX, ATZ, and THIA, indicating its strong applicability. This study provides an example of the development of efficient ternary composite photocatalysts for PMS activation toward organic pollutants removal in the wastewater, but the material preparation was more complex and difficult to be widely used in actual wastewater treatment. In the future, the use of silver ions dissolved in the material for sterilization and algae control while treating organic pollutants in wastewater can be further investigated.

This work was supported by the Major Scientific and Technological Special Program of Sichuan Province, China (2018SZDZX0027), the Science and Technology Support Project of Sichuan Province, China (22ZDYF0849), and the Strategic Cooperation Project of Sichuan University and Luzhou City, Sichuan Province, China (2020CDLZ-6).
